summary: My family and I used to live in Umpiem Refugee Camp before coming to America. It was a place where we stayed as refugees, and many of my friends were there too. The camp was important to me because it was where I grew up and formed connections with people who later became part of my community in the United States.

We arrived in the United States of America on September 19, 2008, hoping for a better life, freedom, and job opportunities. Moving here was a big change for my family and me, and we made sure to bring cultural clothes and family photos to keep our heritage alive. The U.S. has been both challenging and rewarding as we adjusted to a new language and way of life.

When we first arrived in the United States, my family and I lived in Arizona. It was very difficult because we didn’t speak English and couldn’t find jobs easily. We struggled to pay bills like rent, phone, electric, and water, and there wasn’t much help available to us at that time.

In 2010, we moved to Minnesota because many Karen people live there, and I wanted to be part of a supportive community. When I arrived, I saw many old friends who had also lived with me in Umpiem Refugee Camp. Minnesota gave me a sense of belonging and helped me reconnect with my roots and family.
